meta.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
meta.subtitle: Hand-curated CMS coverage metamodel spanning national diagnostic rules, local therapy coverage, and HGNS coding logic.
meta.reviewMethod: Manual review of current CMS NCD, LCD, and Article records for OSA and hypoglossal nerve stimulation, normalized into evidence variables, policy families, contractor variants, and coding bundles.
meta.focus: Show how a richer abstraction supports pathway, matrix, and contractor-comparison views that are much more useful than raw document bullets.

sourceDocuments.title: Sleep Testing for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)

policyFamilies.label: Sleep testing
policyFamilies.summary: National foundation for which sleep-study modalities can establish OSA in Medicare coverage logic.
normalizedQuestions: What type of sleep test was performed?
Was the study ordered and supervised in a Medicare-valid way?
Is the patient clinically suggestive of OSA before treatment decisions begin?
policyFamilies.machinePayoff: A viewer can treat diagnostic modality as a reusable structured gate instead of duplicating narrative fragments in every downstream therapy.

policyFamilies.label: PAP / CPAP
policyFamilies.summary: National CPAP coverage plus DME-level PAP device logic for thresholds, trial period, device escalation, and accessory codes.
normalizedQuestions: Does the sleep test meet numeric severity thresholds?
If severity is intermediate, is there symptoms/comorbidity support?
Has simple CPAP already been tried before more complex PAP devices are considered?
policyFamilies.machinePayoff: The normalized model separates baseline clinical eligibility from device-specific escalation and coding, which is hard to see in the raw documents.

policyFamilies.label: Oral appliance
policyFamilies.summary: DME local policy that reuses the diagnostic backbone but branches on PAP intolerance or contraindication and a dentist-supplied device.
normalizedQuestions: Does the patient meet OSA severity criteria?
Is PAP not tolerated or contraindicated when severity is high?
Was the device ordered by the treating practitioner and supplied by a licensed dentist?
policyFamilies.machinePayoff: A richer UI can show oral appliance coverage as a branch off the same OSA severity model instead of a separate unconnected policy page.

policyFamilies.label: Surgery
policyFamilies.summary: Local WPS surgical logic that depends on confirmed obstruction phenotype, CPAP failure, and explicit non-covered procedures.
normalizedQuestions: Where is the anatomic obstruction: retropalatal or retrolingual?
Has non-invasive therapy failed or been intolerable?
Is the requested surgery one Medicare treats as effective versus investigational?
policyFamilies.machinePayoff: The metamodel can expose which evidence items actually route the patient to different procedure families and which requests should be denied up front.

policyFamilies.label: Hypoglossal nerve stimulation
policyFamilies.summary: Multi-MAC local policy family with a highly consistent clinical core, extensive contraindication screen, explicit DISE workflow, and rich coding articles.
normalizedQuestions: Does the patient fit the clinical core: adult, BMI under 35, PSG within 24 months, AHI 15-65, central/mixed under 25%?
Is there documented CPAP failure or intolerance plus shared decision making?
Do DISE, anatomy, contraindication, provider, and coding requirements all line up?
policyFamilies.machinePayoff: Once normalized, HGNS can be shown as a contractor-comparison problem with stable common gates and a few meaningful local differences, rather than seven nearly identical LCDs plus seven article tabs.

cells.value: 12-week benefit window; E0470 after E0601 failure
Coverage depends on benefit and valid testing
PAP intolerance or PAP contraindication branch
Ordered by treating practitioner, billed by dentist
Documented CPAP failure or intolerance + SDM
Qualified surgeon, sleep physician, accredited sleep lab

evidenceVariables.label: Clinical symptoms and signs of OSA
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: National sleep-testing coverage begins with a symptomatic patient rather than a test-first workflow.

evidenceVariables.label: Sleep-test modality
evidenceVariables.capture: Type I / II / III / IV or equivalent device category
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: The modality determines whether the diagnostic study can support downstream Medicare coverage.

evidenceVariables.label: AHI / RDI severity
evidenceVariables.capture: Numeric events per hour from PSG or qualifying sleep study
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: This is the reusable severity axis for PAP, oral appliance, surgery, and HGNS.

evidenceVariables.label: Symptoms or qualifying comorbidities
evidenceVariables.capture: Daytime sleepiness / cognition / insomnia / mood or hypertension / IHD / stroke history
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: PAP and oral appliance policies use the same intermediate-severity branch rather than a single hard AHI threshold.

evidenceVariables.label: Treating-practitioner evaluation
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: This is a reusable Medicare workflow prerequisite across PAP and oral appliance branches.

evidenceVariables.label: CPAP failure or intolerance
evidenceVariables.capture: Structured adherence / benefit evidence plus narrative shared decision documentation
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: This is the major pivot from standard therapy into oral appliance, surgery, or HGNS escalation.

evidenceVariables.label: Age
evidenceVariables.capture: Years at time of implant decision
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: HGNS policies are explicitly adult-only and use a fixed age threshold.

evidenceVariables.label: Body mass index (BMI)
evidenceVariables.capture: kg/m², often also reflected as Z68 diagnosis coding
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: HGNS uses BMI both as a clinical threshold and as secondary diagnosis coding logic.

evidenceVariables.label: PSG recency
evidenceVariables.capture: Date of PSG relative to implant consultation
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: HGNS makes recency explicit instead of relying on any historical OSA diagnosis.

evidenceVariables.label: Central / mixed apnea share
evidenceVariables.capture: Percent of total AHI that is central or mixed
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: HGNS requires predominantly obstructive disease and turns this into a bright-line exclusion.

evidenceVariables.label: DISE collapse pattern
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: HGNS implant candidacy depends on the absence of complete concentric collapse at the soft palate.

evidenceVariables.label: Anatomic obstruction phenotype
evidenceVariables.capture: Retropalatal / retrolingual / enlarged tongue / other anatomic abnormality
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: Surgery depends on obstruction phenotype, while HGNS excludes anatomy that compromises the device.

evidenceVariables.label: Provider and facility qualification
evidenceVariables.capture: Board status, manufacturer training, accredited sleep facility, DISE proficiency
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: The richest HGNS LCDs operationalize provider capability as part of coverage readiness.

evidenceVariables.label: MRI / device compatibility
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: The richer HGNS local policies push device-model safety into the decision path.

evidenceVariables.label: Claim-ready code bundle
evidenceVariables.capture: Procedure codes + diagnosis logic + bill/revenue artifacts
evidenceVariables.whyItMatters: Articles translate the clinical model into a claim structure, especially for HGNS and surgery.

commonEligibility.label: PSG within 24 months of initial implant consultation
Predominantly obstructive disease with central/mixed apnea under 25% of total AHI
Documented CPAP failure or CPAP intolerance
DISE confirms no complete concentric collapse at the soft palate
No anatomy that would compromise the device, such as large tonsils

expandedContraindications: Central and mixed apneas exceed one-quarter of total AHI
Severe restrictive or obstructive pulmonary disease
New York Heart Association class III or IV heart failure
Recent myocardial infarction or severe cardiac arrhythmia within 6 months
Persistent uncontrolled hypertension despite medication use
Active serious mental illness or acute psychiatric disease interfering with device operation
Coexisting non-respiratory sleep disorders that confound functional assessment
Inability to operate the external sleep remote or lack of needed assistance
Condition or procedure compromising neurologic control of the upper airway
MRI incompatibility for the specific implanted device model

operations.label: Shared decision workflow
operations.detail: The mature HGNS policies treat CPAP failure or intolerance as a documented shared-decision event, not just a narrative complaint.

operations.label: DISE validation
operations.detail: Palmetto and WPS are explicit that DISE is operational, not just diagnostic. The inserting provider must be able to support DISE interpretation quality and documentation.

operations.label: FDA-approved device requirement
operations.detail: The raw indication sections push device approval status into the covered-versus-noncovered distinction instead of leaving it as background context.

operations.label: Qualified implant ecosystem
operations.detail: The richer HGNS LCDs define a full ecosystem: qualified otolaryngologist, BE/BC sleep physician, accredited sleep facility, and manufacturer training.

normalizedCodingBundle.primaryDiagnosisPattern: G47.33 obstructive sleep apnea is the anchor diagnosis in every current HGNS article reviewed.
normalizedCodingBundle.secondaryDiagnosisPattern: Most MAC families express BMI under 35 as a secondary diagnosis requirement using Z68.* BMI codes.

macPolicies.alignment: Current clinical core aligned with the broad MAC pattern.
highlights: Article includes bill and revenue rows

macPolicies.alignment: Clinical core aligned; notable for JE/JF consolidation and the largest diagnosis table in this prototype.
Clinical core aligned; raw indication text is especially explicit about DISE validation, SDM, and implant ecosystem requirements.
highlights: Explicit DISE validation and SDM expectations
documentationFocus: Pairs with broader raw LCD workflow language

macPolicies.alignment: Clinical core aligned; most explicit local variant for SDM mechanics, CPAP usage measurement, device approval, and credentialing.
highlights: Most detailed provider and DISE proficiency language

macPolicies.alignment: Earlier HGNS policy date, but still matches the common clinical core closely.
highlights: Lean article with no bill/revenue rows

macPolicies.alignment: Older HGNS policy date, but rich article tables make it a strong coding reference.
highlights: Full article with bill and revenue rows

macPolicies.alignment: Very similar to FCSO on both clinical core and coding bundle shape.
highlights: Article includes bill and revenue rows
Diagnosis and CPT support mirrors FCSO closely

layeringModels.title: Diagnostic floor, then local narrowing
layeringModels.baselineSummary: NCD 240.4.1 establishes the Medicare-valid sleep-test modalities that can feed downstream OSA coverage decisions.

baselineCriteria.text: Clinical signs and symptoms suggestive of OSA must be present before testing.
Type I attended polysomnography is covered in a sleep lab.
Type II and Type III studies can be attended or unattended and may occur outside the lab.
Type IV devices are covered when they meet the required channel and airflow characteristics.

layeringModels.overlaySummary: There is no standalone sleep-testing LCD. Instead, downstream therapy LCDs inherit the national floor and add stricter workflow rules for their own branch.

overlayCriteria.text: PAP coverage adds an in-person clinical evaluation before the sleep test and minimum event-count math for severity branches.
The surgery path requires diagnosis in an AASM-certified sleep laboratory rather than any qualifying home-study pathway.
HGNS LCDs require PSG within 24 months of implant consultation, converting the national diagnostic floor into a recency gate.

layeringModels.note: This is the clearest example of a reusable baseline. The national rule defines what counts as a valid sleep study; each downstream therapy branch then narrows how fresh, supervised, or facility-bound that study must be.

layeringModels.title: True NCD-to-LCD layering
layeringModels.baselineSummary: NCD 240.4 sets the national CPAP floor: adult OSA, qualifying sleep testing, severity thresholds, education, and an initial trial period.

baselineCriteria.text: Adult patient with obstructive sleep apnea.
Positive diagnosis using Medicare-covered PSG or Type II-IV testing.
AHI or RDI at least 15 events per hour qualifies directly.
AHI or RDI 5-14 requires symptoms or qualifying comorbidities.
Initial 12-week trial plus beneficiary education on device use.

layeringModels.overlaySummary: The DME MAC PAP LCD does what local policy should do well: it does not change the disease concept, but it sharply operationalizes coverage continuation, escalation, supplies, and ordering rules.

overlayCriteria.text: In-person treating-practitioner evaluation must occur before the sleep test.
The LCD translates the NCD trial into a 90-day adherence window with objective use thresholds and re-evaluation between days 31 and 91.
E0470 becomes an escalation path only after documented E0601 failure, and E0471 is explicitly not covered for OSA.
The LCD adds Standard Written Order, prior-to-delivery, refill, and replacement rules that are absent from the NCD text.

layeringModels.note: This is the best codified example of Medicare layering in the OSA stack: the NCD carries the clinical floor, and the LCD adds operational constraints, equipment branching, and supply logic.

layeringModels.title: National analog plus local branch logic
layeringModels.baselineSummary: There is no oral-appliance NCD, but the branch still inherits the national OSA diagnostic backbone and the same mild-versus-moderate severity logic used in CPAP coverage.

baselineCriteria.text: OSA must be established with a Medicare-valid sleep study.
The same AHI or RDI 5-14 plus symptom/comorbidity branch remains relevant.

layeringModels.overlaySummary: The oral-appliance LCD defines where this alternative device sits in the pathway: mild to moderate disease can proceed directly, while higher-severity use depends on PAP intolerance or contraindication.

overlayCriteria.text: For severe OSA, coverage pivots on PAP intolerance or PAP contraindication rather than pure disease severity.
Custom-fabricated E0486 is the covered device lane; prefabricated and tongue-positioning appliances are not covered.
The appliance must be ordered by the treating practitioner and supplied and billed by a licensed dentist.

layeringModels.note: Oral appliance coverage behaves like a local path layered onto the national OSA severity model, even though there is no dedicated national oral-appliance policy.

layeringModels.title: LCD-only pathway built on national analogs
layeringModels.baselineSummary: HGNS has no NCD. The closest inherited floor is national OSA diagnosis plus the idea that CPAP is first-line therapy before escalation.

baselineCriteria.text: OSA must be established with a Medicare-valid sleep study and quantified by AHI or RDI.
CPAP remains the national first-line therapy reference point, creating the practical expectation of documented failure or intolerance before implant escalation.

layeringModels.overlaySummary: The MAC LCDs turn HGNS into a tight, computable local pathway: adult-only, BMI ceiling, AHI window, recent PSG, DISE anatomy rules, contraindications, provider qualifications, and coding expectations.

overlayCriteria.text: Age 22 or older.
BMI under 35 kg/m².
AHI 15-65 with central and mixed apneas under 25% of total AHI.
PSG within 24 months of implant consultation.
DISE must show no complete concentric collapse at the soft palate and no anatomy that compromises the device.
Qualified implant ecosystem: trained otolaryngologist, sleep physician, accredited sleep facility, and DISE proficiency expectations.

layeringModels.note: For HGNS, the right comparison is not literal NCD inheritance but a national OSA foundation overlaid with a very specific local implant pathway.

layeringModels.title: Escalation surgery layered on diagnostic and CPAP failure analogs
layeringModels.baselineSummary: Surgery has no NCD, but the branch still inherits the national OSA diagnostic floor and the practical expectation that non-invasive treatment should come first.

baselineCriteria.text: OSA must be established with a Medicare-valid sleep study before any procedural escalation is considered.
CPAP remains the reference first-line therapy, so surgery enters the pathway only after documented failure or intolerance of non-invasive treatment.

layeringModels.overlaySummary: The WPS surgery LCD turns escalation into a phenotype-driven pathway: certified-lab diagnosis, RDI threshold, site-specific obstruction evidence, covered procedure families, and explicit non-covered procedures.

overlayCriteria.text: Diagnosis must come from an AASM-certified sleep laboratory rather than any broader home-study pathway.
RDI must be at least 15 events per hour.
Requested surgery must match the documented obstruction phenotype, such as retropalatal, retrolingual, or tongue-base disease.

The branch is procedure-selective: UPPP, maxillomandibular advancement, hyoid suspension, tongue-base suspension, tracheostomy, and turbinate reduction are covered, while LAUP, Somnoplasty, palatal implants, and tongue-base ablation are not.

layeringModels.note: Surgery is not just another second-line option. The LCD makes it a structured escalation branch where anatomy and requested procedure family matter as much as disease severity.

families.title: PAP device and accessory atlas
families.summary: L33718 exposes both core equipment branching and the refill-governed supply stack, which makes the PAP LCD much more operational than the NCD alone.

groups.label: Core equipment
groups.note: E0601 is first-line. E0470 is an escalation path after documented E0601 failure. E0471 is explicitly not covered for OSA.

rows.description: Bi-level pressure device without backup rate
rows.notes: Used only after documented E0601 failure

rows.description: Bi-level pressure device with backup rate

groups.label: Accessory stack
groups.note: These supply and humidifier codes are listed directly in the LCD HCPCS table and are governed by refill and replacement rules.

rows.description: Replacement oral cushion for combination mask
Replacement nasal pillows for combination mask

groups.label: Operational modifiers
groups.note: These modifiers and ordering rules are part of why the PAP LCD is more operationally specific than the NCD.

rows.description: No physician or other licensed practitioner order
Waiver of liability statement on file
Item expected to be denied as not reasonable and necessary
Requirements specified in the medical policy have been met

families.title: Oral appliance device lane
families.summary: The oral-appliance LCD is compact but explicit about which HCPCS lanes are covered versus denied.

groups.label: Device codes
groups.note: The custom-fabricated route is the covered lane; prefabricated or miscellaneous alternatives are not supported.

rows.description: Custom-fabricated oral device to reduce upper-airway collapsibility

rows.notes: Denied due to insufficient evidence in the LCD
Used for the non-covered tongue-positioning path

families.title: Surgery code atlas
families.summary: The WPS surgery article is where the clinical obstruction model becomes concrete CPT and ICD-10 branching.

groups.label: Procedure codes
groups.note: Covered and non-covered procedure families are visible in a single article code table.

rows.description: LeFort I reconstruction without bone graft
LeFort I reconstruction requiring bone graft
Mandibular rami or body reconstruction with internal fixation
Tracheostomy with fenestration and skin flaps

rows.notes: Used when billing LAUP or Somnoplasty for denial purposes

groups.label: Diagnosis linkage
groups.note: The article makes diagnosis-to-procedure pairing explicit, especially for tongue-base procedures.

families.title: HGNS claim model
families.summary: The HGNS articles are highly structured and expose the cleanest claim-ready view in the entire OSA prototype: procedure trio, primary diagnosis anchor, and BMI secondary-diagnosis pattern.

groups.label: Procedure trio
groups.note: The implant, revision, and removal codes are stable across the current HGNS article set.

rows.description: Open implantation of HGNS array, pulse generator, and distal respiratory sensor
Revision or replacement of HGNS array and distal respiratory sensor
Removal of HGNS array, pulse generator, and distal respiratory sensor
rows.notes: Some articles note that removal does not always require the dual-diagnosis pattern

groups.label: Diagnosis pattern
groups.note: Every article reviewed anchors HGNS claims on G47.33, then adds BMI coding as a secondary pattern with contractor-specific ceiling differences.

rows.description: Common BMI secondary-diagnosis range used by Palmetto, FCSO, and Novitas
rows.notes: Aligns cleanly with the clinical BMI < 35 rule

rows.description: Noridian-only article extension above the clinical BMI ceiling
rows.notes: Illustrates drift between billing table breadth and clinical LCD text

groups.label: Workflow companion
groups.note: Some of the highest-value HGNS structure is procedural workflow rather than a billable implant code: DISE evidence, shared decision-making, and do-not-bill-together guardrails.

rows.description: National Correct Coding Initiative edits apply
rows.notes: Articles explicitly warn against incompatible billing combinations

rows.description: Modifier 52 for partial revision or component removal
rows.notes: Appears in Palmetto and Noridian article logic

hgnsMacVariation.title: HGNS BMI coding drift by MAC
hgnsMacVariation.summary: Clinical LCDs consistently say BMI under 35. The billing articles are mostly aligned, but Noridian's diagnosis table extends beyond that clinical ceiling.

rows.implication: Article diagnosis table reaches above the clinical ceiling.
Billing table aligns with the clinical LCD threshold.
Billing table aligns with the clinical LCD threshold.
Billing table aligns with the clinical LCD threshold.

insights.title: OSA coverage is layered, not document-centric
insights.body: National policy handles the diagnosis and CPAP foundation, while local policies branch into device, oral appliance, surgery, and HGNS pathways. A richer UI should expose that stack directly instead of forcing a reviewer to infer it from separate documents.

insights.title: The same evidence variables recur across therapies
insights.body: AHI/RDI, valid sleep testing, PAP failure, BMI, and obstruction phenotype reappear across multiple policy families. Normalizing those variables enables much better cross-document navigation and reuse.

insights.title: HGNS is mostly a stable core plus a few meaningful local deltas
insights.body: Across seven current MAC-family HGNS LCDs, the clinical core is highly consistent. The real local differences are how explicitly they operationalize contraindications, DISE validation, provider qualification, and coding detail.

insights.title: Articles are where claim logic becomes computable
insights.body: The HGNS articles translate narrative clinical policy into procedure codes, primary and secondary diagnosis patterns, and sometimes bill or revenue rows. That coding layer deserves its own visualization instead of being buried in a detail accordion.

disease.label: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
disease.definition: Collapse of oropharyngeal walls causing airflow obstruction during sleep, measured by AHI (apnea-hypopnea index) or RDI (respiratory disturbance index) events per hour.

clinicalDefinitions.apnea: Cessation of airflow for at least 10 seconds
clinicalDefinitions.hypopnea: Abnormal respiratory event lasting ≥10 seconds with ≥30% reduction in airflow and ≥4% oxygen desaturation
clinicalDefinitions.ahi: Average number of apnea + hypopnea episodes per hour of sleep
clinicalDefinitions.rdi: Average number of respiratory disturbances per hour of recording time

treatmentPathway.description: Medicare OSA treatment follows a stepped pathway. Sleep testing establishes diagnosis, then CPAP/PAP is first-line therapy. Second-line options (HGNS, oral appliances, surgery) require documented CPAP failure or intolerance.

steps.label: Sleep Testing & Diagnosis
steps.description: Type I–IV sleep studies to establish OSA diagnosis and AHI/RDI baseline

steps.label: CPAP / PAP Therapy
steps.description: First-line treatment: 12-week trial with objective adherence monitoring (≥4 hrs/night, 70% of nights)

steps.label: Second-Line Treatments
steps.description: If CPAP fails or is not tolerated, three paths diverge based on patient anatomy, severity, and BMI

treatmentModels.label: Sleep Testing for OSA

ncd.title: Sleep Testing for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)

otherRequirements: Clinical signs and symptoms indicative of OSA

coveredMethods.label: Type I PSG
coveredMethods.description: Attended facility-based polysomnography (≥7 channels: EEG, EOG, EMG, ECG, airflow, effort, SaO2)

coveredMethods.label: Type IV Device
coveredMethods.description: ≥3 channels with at least one airflow channel

coveredMethods.label: Actigraphy + Oximetry + PAT
coveredMethods.description: Multi-channel with actigraphy, oximetry, peripheral arterial tone

notCovered: Other diagnostic sleep tests not meeting Type I–IV criteria

treatmentModels.label: CPAP / PAP Devices

ncd.title: Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) Therapy For Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)

lcds.title: Positive Airway Pressure (PAP) Devices for the Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea

otherRequirements: In-person clinical evaluation before sleep test
Objective adherence monitoring (≥4 hrs/night, 70% of nights in 30-day period)

trialRequirements.failureConsequence: Coverage denied after day 91 if criteria not met

notCovered: E0471 bi-level with backup rate for OSA diagnosis

criteria.label: Core eligibility
requirements: Central + mixed apneas < 25% of total AHI
Documented CPAP failure (AHI >15 despite use) or intolerance (<4 hrs/night, 5 nights/week)
DISE showing no complete concentric collapse at soft palate
No other anatomical compromise (e.g., tonsil size 3–4)

contraindications: Neuromuscular disease or hypoglossal nerve palsy
Recent MI or severe arrhythmias (< 6 months)
providerRequirements: Completed residency/fellowship + FDA manufacturer training (classroom + cadaver)
DISE surgeon must have 80% agreement certification (≥15 studies)

procedureCodes.label: Open implantation of HGNS array, pulse generator, and respiratory sensor
Revision/replacement of HGNS array and respiratory sensor
Removal of HGNS array, pulse generator, and respiratory sensor

icd10Required.secondary: BMI code from Z68.1 through Z68.34 (BMI 19.9–34.9)

otherRequirements: In-person evaluation prior to sleep test
Device ordered after sleep test review
Must be provided and billed by licensed dentist (DDS or DMD)

criteria.label: RDI ≥ 15 + CPAP failure + anatomic obstruction
requirements: OSA diagnosed in AASM-certified sleep lab

procedures.label: Maxillomandibular Advancement
procedures.indication: Retrolingual obstruction or prior UPPP failure
procedures.notes: With/without genioglossus advancement and hyoid suspension

procedures.label: Tongue Base Suspension
procedures.indication: Tongue base obstruction with macroglossia or hypertrophy

procedures.label: Tracheostomy
procedures.indication: OSA unresponsive to all other treatments
procedures.notes: Most effective surgical approach; reserved for failure of other methods

procedures.label: Turbinate Reduction
procedures.indication: Nasal obstruction due to turbinate hypertrophy contributing to OSA or compromising CPAP

procedures.label: Somnoplasty
procedures.notes: Not proven effective; bill as unlisted procedure

perMacCodeTables.description: ICD-10 codes accepted per MAC for HGNS billing, showing variation in BMI code range

codingNotes: Modifier 52 for revision of single lead only
Dual diagnosis required: G47.33 + BMI code
64584 removal does not require dual diagnosis
Dual diagnosis required: G47.33 + BMI code
LAUP is not a covered service — no CPT code listed
42299 used for unlisted palate procedures when non-covered procedures are performed

crossMacComparison.description: The 8 HGNS LCDs share identical core clinical criteria (coordinated via simultaneous CAC meetings in June 2019). The meaningful differences are in coding/billing articles, operational wording, and MAC-specific structures — not in the clinical coverage rules.
crossMacComparison.verdictSummary: Clinical criteria: aligned across all MACs. Real differences: billing code ranges, coding guidance text, documentation requirements, and LCD structure.

bmiCodeVariation.description: Some MACs accept BMI codes up to Z68.39 (BMI 39.9) while the clinical LCD threshold is BMI < 35. This creates a discrepancy between the LCD text and the billing article code list.

realDifferences.description: Noridian accepts BMI codes Z68.35–Z68.39 (BMI 35.0–39.9) while all other MACs stop at Z68.34 (BMI 34.9). All LCD texts require BMI < 35, creating an adjudication gap at Noridian.
realDifferences.detail: Noridian: 22 ICD-10 covered codes (Z68.1–Z68.39). Palmetto/First Coast/Novitas: 17 codes (Z68.1–Z68.34). Five extra BMI codes at Noridian.

realDifferences.description: Noridian JF legacy article (A57949, now retired) included CPT 64568 (cranial nerve neurostimulator) in addition to the standard 64582/64583/64584. Removed in JE consolidated article.
realDifferences.detail: CPT 64568 was a pre-existing vagus nerve stimulator code presumably used before dedicated HGNS codes were created.

realDifferences.description: Only Noridian articles explicitly prohibit billing 64582+64583, 64582+64584, or 64583+64584 together. Other MACs omit this coding guidance.
realDifferences.detail: Palmetto, First Coast, and Novitas do not include these 'do not bill X with Y' rules.

realDifferences.description: Noridian and Palmetto include modifier 52 guidance for partial revision/removal. First Coast and Novitas omit modifier 52 guidance entirely.
realDifferences.detail: Noridian: 'Use modifier 52 for revision of either the electrode array or respiratory sensor.' Palmetto: 'Append modifier 52 in instances where only a portion of the device is revised.' First Coast/Novitas: silent.

realDifferences.description: Only Palmetto includes extensive Advance Beneficiary Notice modifier guidance (-GA, -GX, -GY, -GZ) and referring/ordering physician NPI requirements.
realDifferences.detail: This is unique to Palmetto's billing article (A58075) and absent from all other MAC articles.

realDifferences.description: Only First Coast and Novitas mention CPT 61886 (cranial neurostimulator pulse generator replacement) in their coding guidance text, though it is not in any code table.
realDifferences.detail: 61886 is referenced as an alternative code for pulse generator replacement. Other MACs do not mention this code.

realDifferences.description: Palmetto's HGNS LCD (L38276) is structurally different from all other HGNS LCDs: it uses a different CMS Coverage Policy citation format, merges AHI criteria into the PSG bullet, names the Inspire device explicitly, and uses a completely different evidence summary structure.
realDifferences.detail: Palmetto LCD is ~5,500 chars vs ~10,800 for others. It omits the anatomy background section, uses SSA title citations instead of IOM references, and its analysis of evidence says 'Level of evidence — Good (I-IIb)' instead of the paragraph-form analysis used by others.

realDifferences.description: Palmetto uniquely adds a 70% threshold to CPAP intolerance: 'usage less than 4 hours a night for at least 70% of the nights in 1 month.' Other MACs define intolerance as '<4 hours/night, 5 nights/week' with no percentage.
realDifferences.detail: This is a materially different clinical criterion despite the shared 4-hour threshold. Palmetto's 70% over 1 month is a different measurement window than '5 nights/week.'

realDifferences.description: WPS defines shared decision-making (SDM) as documented by both the referring and implanting physician. All other MACs define SDM as 'any documented conversation between an attending provider and the patient, and not between multiple providers.'
realDifferences.detail: WPS: 'SDM shall be documented in the patient's record by the referring physician and the implanting physician.' Others: SDM is a patient-provider conversation only.

realDifferences.description: Some MACs accept 'board eligible' in addition to 'board certified' for the implanting otolaryngologist. Others require board certification only.
realDifferences.detail: WPS/CGS/Novitas: 'board certified otolaryngologist.' Noridian/First Coast/NGS add 'or a board eligible.'

realDifferences.description: Each MAC references a different local sleep study LCD/article for the PSG requirement.
realDifferences.detail: WPS: L36839; CGS: L36902; Noridian JE: L36861; Noridian JF: L34040; NGS: A53019 (article, not LCD); First Coast: L33405; Novitas: L35050.

realDifferences.description: L38312 (Noridian JF) was retired 2025-10-16 and consolidated into L38310 (Noridian JE). It is the only retired HGNS LCD.
realDifferences.detail: History section states: 'This final LCD is being retired and combined into the JE LCD.'

realDifferences.description: NGS uses shorter, restructured limitation wording and uniquely adds 'affecting the respiratory system' to the neuromuscular disease contraindication.
realDifferences.detail: NGS: 'Neuromuscular disease affecting the respiratory system.' Others: 'Neuromuscular disease' (unqualified).

realDifferences.description: Palmetto is the only HGNS LCD missing the 'associated_info' section. Only Noridian JF has a 'history_exp' (revision history) section. The 'issue' section is present in only 4 of 8 LCDs.
realDifferences.detail: Issue section present: WPS, CGS, Noridian JE, Palmetto. Absent: Noridian JF, NGS, First Coast, Novitas.

realDifferences.description: Palmetto's billing article requires clinically specific documentation (symptoms, polysomnography, DISE, BMI). All other MACs use generic record-keeping language.
realDifferences.detail: Palmetto A58075 criteria: 'Symptoms', 'Polysomnography reporting', 'Drug Induced Sleep Endoscopy (DISE) reporting-CPT code 42975', 'Body mass index (BMI).' Others: 'All documentation must be maintained in the patient's medical record.'

realDifferences.description: WPS references 13 related documents (uniquely including polysomnography LCDs, Category III code articles, and IDTF articles). CGS references only 3.
realDifferences.detail: WPS uniquely references L35490 (Category III Codes), L36839 (Polysomnography), A54953 (IDTF). Most MACs reference 5–6 related documents.

policyTimeline.event: NCD 240.4: Initial CPAP coverage for moderate/severe OSA
NCD 240.4: Home sleep testing found insufficient — remains non-covered
NCD 240.4: Expanded to include home sleep testing (Type II–IV) for CPAP qualification
NCD 240.4.1: National coverage established for sleep testing devices
Multiple MACs finalize HGNS LCDs after coordinated CAC reviews (June 2019)
Noridian, First Coast, Novitas HGNS LCDs effective
CGS PAP Devices LCD updated for CMS refill requirements
WPS Surgical Treatment LCD biennial review (no changes)
WPS HGNS LCD updated (L38528 v9) — most recent

procedural.description: Open implantation HGNS array + pulse generator + respiratory sensor
Removal HGNS array + pulse generator + respiratory sensor
LeFort I reconstruction, single piece, without bone graft
LeFort I reconstruction, single piece, with bone graft
Mandibular rami reconstruction, sagittal split, internal fixation
Ablation soft tissue inferior turbinates, intramural

device.description: Bi-level PAP with backup rate (NOT COVERED for OSA)

abstractionLayers.label: Requirement dictionary
abstractionLayers.description: Canonical requirement entities with definitions, evidence payloads, and reuse across policy families.

abstractionLayers.label: Document rule packs
abstractionLayers.description: Each NCD/LCD/article decomposed into typed requirement statements with semantic relations to the baseline.

abstractionLayers.label: Lineage matrix
abstractionLayers.description: Family-by-family comparison showing whether the policy baseline is reused, narrowed, operationalized, added to, or genuinely different.

abstractionLayers.label: Contractor variance
abstractionLayers.description: Fine-grained contractor differences where nominally similar LCDs diverge in wording, provider rules, or billing articles.

relationLegend.label: National baseline
relationLegend.meaning: The NCD establishes the canonical Medicare rule for this requirement.

relationLegend.label: Reuses baseline
relationLegend.meaning: The LCD inherits the same underlying requirement without changing the disease concept.

relationLegend.label: Narrows
relationLegend.meaning: The local policy constrains an inherited rule with a stricter test, site, timing, or patient-selection gate.

relationLegend.label: Operationalizes
relationLegend.meaning: The local policy turns a broad national concept into auditable workflow or documentation thresholds.

relationLegend.label: Adds branch logic
relationLegend.meaning: The local policy adds a therapy-specific requirement that has no direct national counterpart.

relationLegend.label: Literal difference
relationLegend.meaning: Two local documents treat the same requirement differently enough to matter analytically.

relationLegend.label: Coding layer
relationLegend.meaning: The rule appears in billing/article logic rather than in the main coverage criteria text.

requirementGroups.label: Diagnosis And Severity
requirementGroups.description: How OSA is established, how severe it must be, and how measurements are interpreted.

requirementGroups.label: Pathway And Step Therapy
requirementGroups.description: When prior treatment failure, intolerance, or escalation becomes part of coverage logic.

requirementGroups.label: Workflow And Documentation
requirementGroups.description: Operational rules that turn broad coverage concepts into auditable steps.

requirementGroups.label: Provider And Site
requirementGroups.description: Which clinicians, facilities, or special studies are required.

requirementGroups.label: Device And Procedure
requirementGroups.description: What hardware, procedure lanes, and exclusions the policy recognizes.

requirementGroups.label: Coding And Adjudication
requirementGroups.description: What articles, code ranges, modifiers, and billing constraints operationalize the decision.

requirementCatalog.label: Clinical signs and symptoms
requirementCatalog.definition: Whether the beneficiary has clinical signs, symptoms, or findings that justify OSA testing or treatment coverage.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for clinical signs and symptoms?

requirementCatalog.label: Treating practitioner order
requirementCatalog.definition: Whether a qualified treating practitioner ordered the sleep study or downstream therapy.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for treating practitioner order?

requirementCatalog.label: Valid sleep-test modality
requirementCatalog.definition: Which sleep-study modalities qualify as valid Medicare diagnostic evidence for OSA coverage decisions.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for valid sleep-test modality?

requirementCatalog.label: Sleep-test site restriction
requirementCatalog.definition: Whether the policy accepts any qualifying Medicare sleep test or narrows to lab-only or PSG-only pathways.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for sleep-test site restriction?

requirementCatalog.label: Sleep-study recency
requirementCatalog.definition: How recent the diagnostic study must be relative to the treatment decision.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for sleep-study recency?

requirementCatalog.label: Adult age threshold
requirementCatalog.definition: Age requirement for treatment eligibility, including adult-only coverage or explicit minimum age.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for adult age threshold?

requirementCatalog.label: AHI/RDI severity threshold
requirementCatalog.definition: Apnea-hypopnea or respiratory disturbance thresholds that determine whether the disease is severe enough for a branch of coverage.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for ahi/rdi severity threshold?

requirementCatalog.label: Mild OSA comorbidity branch
requirementCatalog.definition: Whether mild OSA can qualify only when paired with symptoms or qualifying comorbidities.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for mild osa comorbidity branch?

requirementCatalog.label: Obstructive predominance limit
requirementCatalog.definition: Upper bound on central or mixed apneas as a share of total AHI when selecting patients for implant therapy.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for obstructive predominance limit?

requirementCatalog.label: BMI upper limit
requirementCatalog.definition: Maximum body-mass index permitted by the coverage rule.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for bmi upper limit?

requirementCatalog.label: Pre-test in-person evaluation
requirementCatalog.definition: Whether the beneficiary must have an in-person clinical evaluation before the qualifying sleep study.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for pre-test in-person evaluation?

requirementCatalog.label: CPAP failure definition
requirementCatalog.definition: How the policy defines failed CPAP therapy when a downstream treatment requires prior PAP failure.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for cpap failure definition?

requirementCatalog.label: CPAP intolerance definition
requirementCatalog.definition: How the policy defines intolerance to CPAP when alternative therapy becomes coverable.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for cpap intolerance definition?

requirementCatalog.label: Shared decision-making
requirementCatalog.definition: How the policy defines and documents the patient-provider decision process before implant therapy.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for shared decision-making?

requirementCatalog.label: Trial and continuation window
requirementCatalog.definition: The coverage window used to establish initial benefit and continuation of PAP coverage.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for trial and continuation window?

requirementCatalog.label: Objective adherence threshold
requirementCatalog.definition: The machine-derived use threshold that the beneficiary must meet for continued PAP coverage or for some local alternative-treatment definitions.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for objective adherence threshold?

requirementCatalog.label: Clinical re-evaluation window
requirementCatalog.definition: When the treating practitioner must re-evaluate the beneficiary to continue coverage.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for clinical re-evaluation window?

requirementCatalog.label: Anatomic assessment
requirementCatalog.definition: Procedure or clinical assessment that must confirm suitable upper-airway anatomy.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for anatomic assessment?

requirementCatalog.label: Provider role
requirementCatalog.definition: Which clinician roles must evaluate, order, supply, or perform the covered service.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for provider role?

requirementCatalog.label: Board credentialing language
requirementCatalog.definition: Whether the policy requires board-certified specialists only or accepts board-eligible clinicians.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for board credentialing language?

requirementCatalog.label: Sleep lab accreditation
requirementCatalog.definition: Whether the qualifying study or downstream titration must occur in an accredited sleep facility.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for sleep lab accreditation?

requirementCatalog.label: DISE validation requirement
requirementCatalog.definition: Special certification or validation rules that govern the DISE interpretation used for HGNS selection.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for dise validation requirement?

requirementCatalog.label: Device lane
requirementCatalog.definition: Which device types are covered, escalated, or excluded for a therapy branch.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for device lane?

requirementCatalog.label: Explicit non-covered procedures
requirementCatalog.definition: Named procedures or device categories the policy specifically excludes from coverage.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for explicit non-covered procedures?

requirementCatalog.label: Covered procedure lane
requirementCatalog.definition: Which CPT/HCPCS procedure lanes are recognized as the covered procedural implementation of the policy.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for covered procedure lane?

requirementCatalog.label: Primary diagnosis code requirement
requirementCatalog.definition: Which primary diagnosis code anchors claim submission for the covered treatment.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for primary diagnosis code requirement?

requirementCatalog.label: Secondary BMI code ceiling
requirementCatalog.definition: How far the coding article extends the BMI-related ICD code range, and whether it stays aligned to the clinical BMI rule.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for secondary bmi code ceiling?

requirementCatalog.label: Modifier guidance
requirementCatalog.definition: Modifier rules or special billing instructions attached to the therapy.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for modifier guidance?

requirementCatalog.label: Mutual exclusion code rules
requirementCatalog.definition: Do-not-bill-together guidance or mutually exclusive code-pair constraints.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for mutual exclusion code rules?

requirementCatalog.label: Legacy code reference
requirementCatalog.definition: References to legacy or transitional procedure codes that survive in older articles or special MAC language.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for legacy code reference?

requirementCatalog.label: Article documentation specificity
requirementCatalog.definition: Whether the billing article just says 'maintain records' or enumerates specific evidence artifacts that must be present.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for article documentation specificity?

requirementCatalog.label: Cross-reference to local sleep-study policy
requirementCatalog.definition: Which local sleep-testing LCD or article the downstream therapy cross-references for diagnostic validity.
requirementCatalog.canonicalQuestion: What does the policy require for cross-reference to local sleep-study policy?

documentProfiles.title: Sleep Testing for Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
documentProfiles.focus: Defines which sleep studies count as valid Medicare evidence for OSA diagnosis.

statements.valueSummary: Clinical signs and symptoms suggestive of OSA are required before testing.
Testing must be ordered by the treating physician or qualified treating practitioner.
Type I PSG, Type II, Type III, Type IV, and PAT-based multi-channel pathways qualify when technical criteria are met.
Type II-IV pathways may occur attended or unattended outside the lab; Type I PSG is lab-based.

documentProfiles.title: Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) Therapy For Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
documentProfiles.focus: Defines national CPAP entry thresholds and the initial trial concept.

statements.valueSummary: Adult OSA coverage only.
Coverage uses Medicare-valid PSG or Type II-IV testing from the sleep-testing NCD.
AHI/RDI ≥ 15 qualifies directly; AHI/RDI 5-14 qualifies only with symptoms or comorbidities.
Mild OSA requires symptoms or listed comorbidities such as hypertension, ischemic heart disease, or stroke.
Initial coverage limited to a 12-week trial period.
Beneficiary education on device use is part of the covered CPAP pathway.

documentProfiles.title: Positive Airway Pressure (PAP) Devices for the Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea
documentProfiles.focus: Turns the CPAP NCD into auditable continuation, adherence, and device-escalation logic.

statements.valueSummary: In-person treating-practitioner evaluation must occur before the qualifying sleep study.
Diagnostic validity still depends on Medicare-covered sleep-testing pathways.
Retains the NCD severity thresholds rather than redefining OSA severity.
Operationalized as a 90-day continuation window instead of a general 12-week concept.
Objective adherence: ≥4 hours/night on ≥70% of nights in a consecutive 30-day period.
Treating practitioner must re-evaluate between days 31 and 91.
E0470 is only reachable after documented E0601 failure; E0601 remains the base device.
E0471 with backup rate is not covered for OSA.

documentProfiles.title: Oral Appliances for Obstructive Sleep Apnea
documentProfiles.focus: Creates an oral-appliance branch that reuses the national diagnostic floor but adds device- and supplier-specific rules.

statements.valueSummary: In-person evaluation required before the qualifying sleep study.
Diagnosis still depends on a Medicare-valid sleep test.
AHI 5-30 can qualify directly depending on severity branch; AHI >30 triggers a PAP-failure path.
For mild disease, the same symptom/comorbidity branch from PAP logic remains in play.
Severe OSA branch requires PAP intolerance or documented PAP contraindication.
Covered device lane is custom-fabricated mandibular advancement only (E0486).
Prefabricated oral appliances and tongue-positioning devices are excluded.
Device must be supplied and billed by a licensed dentist (DDS or DMD).

documentProfiles.title: Surgical Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
documentProfiles.focus: Uses a stricter, surgery-oriented coverage model than the national PAP NCDs.

statements.valueSummary: Diagnosis must be established in an AASM-recognized certified sleep lab.
AASM-certified facility is explicitly required for the diagnostic work-up.
RDI of 15 or higher required; no mild-comorbidity branch.
Coverage depends on failed CPAP or other appropriate non-invasive treatment.
Procedure selection depends on evidence of retropalatal or combined obstruction site.
Procedure must be performed by a qualified surgeon in an appropriate setting.
Explicitly excludes LAUP, palatal implants, somnoplasty, and tongue-base radiofrequency ablation.

documentProfiles.title: Billing and Coding: Surgical Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA)
documentProfiles.focus: Translates the surgical LCD into code tables and diagnosis alignment.

statements.valueSummary: Defines covered surgical procedure code lanes and diagnosis alignment for the WPS surgery branch.
Billing article expects records sufficient to support procedure choice and OSA diagnosis.

documentProfiles.title: Hypoglossal Nerve Stimulation for the Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea
documentProfiles.focus: One of the current HGNS local policies; clinically aligned with the common implant core but with WPS-specific SDM wording.

statements.valueSummary: Age ≥ 22 years
BMI < 35 kg/m²
PSG within 24 months of implant consultation
Central + mixed apneas < 25% of total AHI
AHI 15-65 events/hour
statements.note: This is not the same severity branch used in the PAP NCD; HGNS creates its own implant-selection window.

statements.valueSummary: DISE must show no complete concentric collapse and no other anatomic compromise
Sleep physician plus implanting otolaryngologist participate in selection and treatment pathway
Primary diagnosis anchored to G47.33 in billing articles
Each MAC cross-references a different local sleep-study LCD/article
CPAP failure defined as AHI > 15 despite CPAP usage.
CPAP intolerance uses the <4 hours/night and 5 nights/week style definition.
SDM must be documented by both the referring physician and implanting physician.
Implanting surgeon described as board-certified otolaryngologist.

documentProfiles.title: Hypoglossal Nerve Stimulation for the Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea
documentProfiles.focus: Clinically aligned HGNS LCD using the common LCD family language.

statements.valueSummary: Age ≥ 22 years
BMI < 35 kg/m²
PSG within 24 months of implant consultation
Central + mixed apneas < 25% of total AHI
AHI 15-65 events/hour
statements.note: This is not the same severity branch used in the PAP NCD; HGNS creates its own implant-selection window.

statements.valueSummary: DISE must show no complete concentric collapse and no other anatomic compromise
Sleep physician plus implanting otolaryngologist participate in selection and treatment pathway
Primary diagnosis anchored to G47.33 in billing articles
Each MAC cross-references a different local sleep-study LCD/article
CPAP failure defined as AHI > 15 despite CPAP usage.
CPAP intolerance uses the <4 hours/night and 5 nights/week style definition.
SDM is documented as a patient-provider conversation, not a multi-provider attestation.
Implanting surgeon described as board-certified otolaryngologist.

documentProfiles.title: Hypoglossal Nerve Stimulation for the Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea
documentProfiles.focus: Current Noridian JE LCD; clinically aligned core with Noridian-specific credentialing and DISE validation language.

statements.valueSummary: Age ≥ 22 years
BMI < 35 kg/m²

Central + mixed apneas < 25% of total AHI
AHI 15-65 events/hour
statements.note: This is not the same severity branch used in the PAP NCD; HGNS creates its own implant-selection window.

statements.valueSummary: DISE must show no complete concentric collapse and no other anatomic compromise
Sleep physician plus implanting otolaryngologist participate in selection and treatment pathway

Each MAC cross-references a different local sleep-study LCD/article
CPAP failure defined as AHI > 15 despite CPAP usage.
CPAP intolerance is <4 hours/night, 5 nights/week, or returned CPAP.
SDM is any documented conversation between attending provider and patient.
DISE interpreter must meet manufacturer second-opinion validation threshold (80% agreement in 15 consecutive studies).
Board-certified or board-eligible otolaryngologist accepted.

documentProfiles.title: Billing and Coding: Hypoglossal Nerve Stimulation for Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea
documentProfiles.focus: WPS claim implementation for HGNS.

statements.valueSummary: CPT 64582/64583/64584 are the live procedure lane.
Claims anchor on G47.33.
BMI code lane aligns to BMI < 35.
Generic article record-retention language.

documentProfiles.title: Billing and Coding: Hypoglossal Nerve Stimulation for the Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea
documentProfiles.focus: Current Noridian JE claim implementation with the largest BMI-coding spread.

statements.valueSummary: CPT 64582/64583/64584 are the live procedure lane.
Claims anchor on G47.33.
BMI code lane extends through Z68.39, beyond the clinical BMI < 35 gate.
Explicit do-not-bill-together rules for 64582/64583/64584 combinations.
Modifier 52 guidance for partial revision/removal.

documentProfiles.title: Billing and Coding: Hypoglossal Nerve Stimulation for the Treatment of Obstructive Sleep Apnea
documentProfiles.focus: Retired Noridian JF billing article retained because it exposes the legacy implant code lane.

statements.valueSummary: CPT 64582/64583/64584 are present.
Legacy CPT 64568 appears in the retired article only.
BMI code lane extends through Z68.39, beyond the clinical BMI < 35 gate.
Explicit do-not-bill-together rules present.

familyDeltaSummaries.takeaway: Pure national baseline. Downstream therapies inherit this diagnostic floor and then narrow it.
PAP LCD does not reinvent OSA; it mostly operationalizes and narrows the national CPAP rule.
Local branch reuses the national diagnostic logic but adds a new device lane and severe-disease PAP step-therapy path.
Surgery is genuinely narrower than the national OSA floor and carries explicit non-covered procedures.
HGNS is mostly a new local implant-selection framework layered on top of the older national OSA diagnostic floor, with some real MAC-level wording and billing variation.

rows.whyItMatters: Defines what counts as valid diagnostic evidence before any therapy branch is even considered.

cells.valueSummary: Type I-IV and PAT-capable multi-channel pathways are the diagnostic floor.
Uses the national Medicare-valid sleep-testing floor.
Uses a Medicare-covered sleep test without redefining the test taxonomy.
Requires lab-based AASM-certified sleep evaluation rather than any qualifying home-study path.
Requires PSG and adds a recency gate, collapsing the broader national test menu.

rows.whyItMatters: Shows where local policies truly diverge from the national severity logic.

cells.valueSummary: Diagnostic NCD itself does not set treatment severity gates.
AHI/RDI ≥15 direct, or 5-14 with symptoms/comorbidities.
5-30 can qualify directly; >30 pushes into PAP failure/intolerance logic.
RDI ≥15 only; no mild-comorbidity branch.
AHI 15-65 plus obstructive-event predominance.

rows.whyItMatters: The downstream pathways use CPAP intolerance differently, and HGNS contractors do not define it identically.

cells.valueSummary: Not part of the diagnostic floor.
Not applicable because PAP is the first-line therapy.
Only matters on the severe-OAS branch or when PAP is contraindicated.
Used as part of failed CPAP/non-invasive treatment pathway.
Core rule exists in every HGNS LCD, but Palmetto uses a 70%-of-nights metric while others use 5 nights/week language.

rows.whyItMatters: A good example of local operationalization versus a different therapy-selection definition.

cells.valueSummary: Not applicable.
Continuation requires ≥4 hours/night on ≥70% of nights plus clinical re-evaluation.
No machine-derived adherence threshold.
No machine-derived adherence threshold.
Appears inside some MACs’ CPAP-intolerance definition rather than as a continuation rule.

rows.whyItMatters: Downstream OSA therapies diverge sharply once anatomy becomes a selection gate.

cells.valueSummary: No anatomy gate.
No anatomy gate.
No formal anatomy gate in the LCD.
Obstruction site identification is required to match the operation to the anatomy.
DISE with no complete concentric collapse plus no other anatomy compromise.

rows.whyItMatters: Much of the real local coverage spread lives in who is allowed to furnish or support the therapy.

cells.valueSummary: Treating practitioner order governs entry.
Treating practitioner handles evaluation, re-evaluation, and DME ordering.
Treating practitioner orders; licensed dentist supplies and bills the device.
Qualified surgeon in an appropriate operative setting.
Sleep physician, implanting otolaryngologist, and DISE-capable surgeon all become relevant actors.

rows.whyItMatters: Shows where billing articles can diverge from the clinical text and create adjudication ambiguity.

cells.valueSummary: No therapy-specific BMI code lane.
No BMI code ceiling in the CPAP NCD/LCD pair.
No BMI coding rule.
No central BMI coding lane in this curated surgery slice.
Noridian billing extends to Z68.39 while other MACs stop at Z68.34.

rows.label: CPAP intolerance wording
rows.takeaway: Every HGNS LCD requires CPAP failure/intolerance, but Palmetto uses a different measurement window than the other MACs.

contractorValues.valueSummary: <4 h/night for 70% of nights in 1 month

rows.label: SDM definition
rows.takeaway: WPS is the outlier; most HGNS LCDs define SDM as a patient-provider conversation, not a dual-physician attestation.

rows.label: Board-eligible wording
rows.takeaway: Some MACs accept board-eligible surgeons while others say board-certified only.

rows.label: BMI coding ceiling
rows.takeaway: The real adjudication spread is in the billing articles: Noridian goes beyond the clinical BMI text.

contractorValues.valueSummary: Article not curated separately; family follows common coding bundle

rows.label: Legacy procedure code
rows.takeaway: Only the retired Noridian JF article still carries CPT 64568.

analyticQuestions.prompt: Does this LCD say anything meaningfully different from the NCD?
analyticQuestions.answer: Answer by separating literal restatement, local operationalization, local narrowing, and truly new therapy-specific rules.

analyticQuestions.prompt: Where does the real operational spread live when LCD core criteria look aligned?
analyticQuestions.answer: Answer by moving from the clinical-core matrix to billing/article-specific requirement entries such as BMI code ceilings, modifier guidance, and legacy code references.

analyticQuestions.prompt: What patient-side evidence or claim artifact satisfies this requirement?
analyticQuestions.answer: Answer from the requirement dictionary, which binds each requirement to typical evidence payloads and related variables.
